elements.sent-2411-001: The proposed approach reduces the size of the data set by a factor of five while preserving the essential clustering characteristics through an innovative spectral compression technique.
elements.sent-2411-002: This enables DBSCAN to run substantially faster without any loss of accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-003: Experiments on real-world data sets, such as USPS, demonstrate the method's capability to achieve this dramatic reduction in data size while maintaining clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-004: Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN) \cite{ester1996density has become a prominent method in the field of clustering, especially for identifying clusters of varying shapes and densities.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Unlike traditional methods such as $k$-means, which assume spherical clusters and require the number of clusters to be specified beforehand, DBSCAN identifies clusters based on the density of data points, allowing it to effectively handle clusters of arbitrary shapes.
elements.sent-2411-006: Additionally, DBSCAN has the ability to distinguish noise from core cluster points, making it particularly suitable for real-world data with outliers.
elements.sent-2411-007: Compared to support vector clustering (SVC), DBSCAN does not rely on high-dimensional mappings or support vectors, but rather operates directly in the input space, making it more suitable density-based scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-008: DBSCAN has gained popularity due to its ability to identify clusters of arbitrary shapes and handle noise effectively, making it well-suited for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, the original DBSCAN algorithm faces significant efficiency and scalability challenges when applied to large-scale datasets.
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, the neighborhood search step of DBSCAN has a \(O(N^2)\) time complexity \cite{jang2019dbscan++ , as it requires pairwise distance calculations to identify core points within a specified radius (\(\varepsilon\)).}
elements.sent-2411-011: This quadratic complexity can become prohibitive in big data environments, where the time required for clustering grows rapidly with the number of data points.
elements.sent-2411-012: Additionally, DBSCAN’s performance degrades with high-dimensional data due to the curse of dimensionality, where distance metrics become less meaningful, further impacting both speed and clustering quality.
elements.sent-2411-013: These limitations restrict DBSCAN’s applicability in emerging big data analytics tasks, necessitating an optimized approach to improve its scalability and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-014: Inpsired by \cite{wang2022improving , in this paper, we propose to fundamentally address the efficiency and scalability issues of DBSCAN through a novel spectrum-preserving data compression method.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Our approach first computes a significantly smaller, compressed data set while preserving the essential clustering properties of the original data, utilizing a nearly-linear time spectral data compression technique.
elements.sent-2411-016: This compressed data set allows us to dramatically reduce the search space for density-based clustering, making DBSCAN feasible for large-scale applications.
elements.sent-2411-017: The compressed data then serves as input for an accelerated DBSCAN algorithm, where the original clustering structure is retained, not only without loss in solution quality.
elements.sent-2411-018: An additional benefit of the proposed method is that users can specify the compression ratio based on their requirements.
elements.sent-2411-019: Given a dataset \( D \) with \( N \) samples \( \mathbf{x _1, \dots, \mathbf{x _N \), the DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) algorithm identifies clusters through two main steps: neighborhood search and cluster expansion.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In the neighborhood search step, DBSCAN defines clusters based on the density of data points.
elements.sent-2411-021: For a given point \(\mathbf{x _i\), the algorithm examines all points within a specified radius \(\varepsilon\) (referred to as the \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood).}
elements.sent-2411-022: A point \(\mathbf{x _i\) is classified as a core point if the number of points within its \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood, including \(\mathbf{x _i\) itself, is at least a minimum threshold \(\text{MinPts}\).}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Points that do not meet this criterion are classified either as border points (if they lie within the \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood of a core point) or as noise if they do not belong to any cluster.
elements.sent-2411-024: Once core points are identified, DBSCAN proceeds to the cluster expansion step.
elements.sent-2411-025: Here, each core point initiates a cluster by linking to other core points within its \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood, iteratively expanding the cluster by including reachable core points and their neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-026: This expansion continues until all reachable points within the density threshold are included in the cluster.
elements.sent-2411-027: The process is repeated for all core points, eventually identifying all clusters in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-028: Formally, DBSCAN can be summarized by the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-029: Identify Core Points: For each point \(\mathbf{x _i\), compute the \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood and count the number of points within it.}
elements.sent-2411-030: If the count meets or exceeds \(\text{MinPts}\), classify \(\mathbf{x _i\) as a core point.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Cluster Expansion: For each core point, form a new cluster and iteratively add all directly reachable core points within \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhoods.
elements.sent-2411-032: Expand the cluster until no more points meet the density-reachability criteria.
elements.sent-2411-033: Classify Border and Noise Points: Points within the \(\varepsilon\)-neighborhood of core points but with fewer than \(\text{MinPts}\) neighbors are classified as border points.
elements.sent-2411-034: Points that do not fall within any cluster are classified as noise.
elements.sent-2411-035: DBSCAN’s main advantage lies in its ability to detect clusters of arbitrary shape and to identify noise.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, it also has limitations, particularly in terms of computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-037: The algorithm’s neighborhood search step has a time complexity of \(\mathbf{O(N^2)}\), as it involves pairwise distance calculations for each point.
elements.sent-2411-038: This quadratic complexity can become prohibitive for large-scale datasets, especially in high-dimensional spaces where distance calculations are computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-039: Consider a graph \( G = (V, E, w) \), where \( V \) represents the set of vertices, \( E \) is the set of edges, and \( w \) is a function assigning positive weights to each edge.
elements.sent-2411-040: The Laplacian matrix of graph \( G \), which is a symmetric diagonally dominant (SDD) matrix, is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{formula_laplacian} L_G(p,q) = \begin{cases} -w(p,q) \text{if } (p,q) \in E, \sum\limits_{(p,t) \in E} w(p,t) \text{if } p = q, 0 \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}\end{equation} According to spectral graph theory \cite{chung1997spectral , the spectrum of the graph Laplacian contains valuable information about the clustering structure of the graph, which is essential for identifying clusters.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{formula_laplacian} L_G(p,q) =  -w(p,q) if  (p,q) \in E, \sum\limits_{(p,t) \in E} w(p,t) if  p = q, 0 otherwise. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: For instance, in artificially generated datasets such as two-moons and two-circles, the clustering results of $k$-means applied directly in the original feature space differ significantly from those in the spectrally-embedded feature space.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the original space, the traditional $k$-means algorithm is unable to correctly identify the clusters due to its reliance on Euclidean distance alone.
elements.sent-2411-043: However, by incorporating spectral information into the feature space, the clustering becomes accurate and aligns with the true underlying structure.
elements.sent-2411-044: Given a dataset of samples, we initially build a $k$-nearest neighbors (k-NN) graph $G$, which captures the fundamental structure or manifold of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-045: The corresponding graph Laplacian matrix $L_G$ can then be derived from $G$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The spectral similarity between any two points in the dataset is calculated through the following steps:.
elements.sent-2411-047: Compute the bottom $K$ eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian that are nontrivial;.
elements.sent-2411-048: Form a matrix $U$ by arranging these $K$ eigenvectors as column vectors;.
elements.sent-2411-049: Each row of $U$ serves as a low-dimensional embedded feature vector for a point;.
elements.sent-2411-050: Define the spectral similarity between two points, $u$ and $v$, as follows \cite{livne2012lean :.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Here, $\mathbf{x _u$ and $\mathbf{x _v$ are the feature vectors of points $u$ and $v$ within the embedded space.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Interpreted statistically, $s_{uv}$ represents the fraction of variance explained by the linear regression of $\mathbf{x _u$ on $\mathbf{x _v$ from a spectral viewpoint.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: A larger value of $s_{uv}$ implies a stronger spectral connection between the points.
elements.sent-2411-054: To reduce the computational burden associated with eigen-decomposition, Gauss-Seidel relaxation can be employed to produce smoothed vectors for embedding features, achieving linear-time complexity \cite{livne2012lean .}
elements.sent-2411-055: By using spectral similarity, we divide the samples into subsets where samples in the same subset exhibit high spectral correlation.
elements.sent-2411-056: The mean of feature vectors within each subset is calculated to create a spectrally representative pseudo-sample, and these pseudo-samples collectively form a compressed version of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-057: If further compression is needed, these representative pseudo-samples can be compressed again.
elements.sent-2411-058: Fig.~\ref{fig:USPS7NN and Fig.~\ref{fig:USPS2X illustrate the manifolds of the original USPS dataset and the compressed dataset, respectively, by projecting their $k$-NN graphs in 2D.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This comparison reveals that the compressed data effectively approximates the original manifold structure.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{USPS2NN.png} \caption{The $k$-NN graph corresponding to the original data set (USPS).\protect\label{fig:USPS7NN}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.2]{USPS2X.png} \caption{The $k$-NN graph corresponding to the compressed data set (USPS).\protect\label{fig:USPS2X}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Then, the DBSCAN process can be applied on the compressed data points to group them into distinct clusters.
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally, each original data point is assigned the same cluster membership as its corresponding compressed point, completing the clustering task.
elements.sent-2411-062: The entire algorithm flow is illustrated as follows: Input: A data set $D$ with $N$ samples $x_1,\dots,x_N \in \mathbb{R ^d$.. Output: Clusters $C_1,\dots,C_k$.}
elements.algorithmic-2411-001: [1] \STATE Construct a $k$-nearest neighbor ($k$-NN) graph $G$ from the input data. \STATE Compute the Laplacian matrix corresponding to graph $G$. \STATE Perform spectrum-preserving data compression on the original data set. \STATE Perform DBSCAN on the compressed data set. \STATE Map the cluster memberships of compressed points back to obtain the clustering result of the original data set.
elements.sent-2411-063: The spectrum-preserving data compression requires $O(|E_G|\log(|V|))$ time, where $|E_G|$ denotes the number of edges in the original graph, and $|V|$ represents the number of data points.
elements.sent-2411-064: Applying DBSCAN on the compressed dataset has a complexity of $O(P^2)$, where $P$ is the number of compressed points.
elements.sent-2411-065: Retrieving the cluster memberships for the original data points has a time complexity of $O(N)$, where $N$ is the total number of samples in the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-066: We use USPS data set to evaluate the method.
elements.sent-2411-067: NMI \cite{strehl2002cluster is used as the metrics.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} 1.5{ \begin{tabular}{ |c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c} Orig DBSCAN Ours(5X reduction) \hline0.5513 0.5517 \end{tabular}} \end{center} \caption{Clustering Quality Results (NMI)} \label{table:acc result}
elements.sent-2411-068: To address the computational challenges of DBSCAN, this study presents a novel spectral data compression approach that creates a significantly smaller data set while preserving the essential spectral properties needed for clustering.
elements.sent-2411-069: Experiments on large-scale, real-world data sets demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly accelerates DBSCAN algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-070: DBSCAN is one of the most important non-parametric unsupervised data analysis tools.
elements.sent-2411-071: By applying DBSCAN to a dataset, two key analytical results can be obtained: (1) clustering data points based on density distribution and (2) identifying outliers in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, the time complexity of the DBSCAN algorithm is $O(n^2 \beta)$, where $n$ is the number of data points and $\beta = O(D)$, with $D$ representing the dimensionality of the data space.
elements.sent-2411-073: As a result, DBSCAN becomes computationally infeasible when both $n$ and $D$ are large.
elements.sent-2411-074: In this paper, we propose a DBSCAN method based on spectral data compression, capable of efficiently processing datasets with a large number of data points ($n$) and high dimensionality ($D$).
elements.sent-2411-075: By preserving only the most critical structural information during the compression process, our method effectively removes substantial redundancy and noise.
elements.sent-2411-076: Consequently, the solution quality of DBSCAN is significantly improved, enabling more accurate and reliable results.
elements.sent-2411-077: One of the most fundamental and widely used tasks in data analysis, especially in practical applications, is partitioning data into different clusters and identifying anomalies within the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-078: DBSCAN is an algorithm that uniquely achieves both tasks in a single run, making it highly popular in both academia and industry.
elements.sent-2411-079: Moreover, compared to other mainstream clustering algorithms, such as k-means, spectral clustering, and deep neural network-based clustering methods, DBSCAN does not require prior specification of the number of clusters in the data.
elements.sent-2411-080: This is a critical advantage when working with previously unseen datasets, as it facilitates a more intuitive understanding of the data.
elements.sent-2411-081: However, DBSCAN also has a notable drawback—its high computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-082: A bottleneck of the original DBSCAN algorithm lies in the need to perform a range query for each data item, i.e., calculating the number of neighbors within the distance \( \varepsilon \).
elements.sent-2411-083: Consequently, the overall time complexity can reach \( O(n^2 \beta) \) in the worst case, where \( n \) is the number of data items and \( \beta \) represents the complexity of computing the distance between two items.
elements.sent-2411-084: For instance, if the data is a set of points in \( \mathbb{R ^D \), \( \beta = O(D) \).}
elements.sent-2411-085: When \( n \) or \( \beta \) is large, the range query process can significantly slow down the execution of DBSCAN.
elements.sent-2411-086: This limitation makes DBSCAN unsuitable for the increasingly common demands of large-scale and high-dimensional data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-087: To address this issue, researchers have devoted considerable effort to accelerating DBSCAN.
elements.sent-2411-088: However, our experiments on benchmark datasets from the National Institute of Standards and Technology and the UC Irvine Machine Learning Repository reveal that existing methods invariably face a trade-off between speed and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-089: None of these approaches fully achieve the ultimate goal of significantly accelerating the algorithm while maintaining the quality of DBSCAN's output.
elements.sent-2411-090: In this paper, a novel method has been proposed for accelerating DBSCAN based on spectrum-preserving data compression, enabling DBSCAN to operate efficiently in very large-scale high-dimensional spaces.
elements.sent-2411-091: This approach paves the way for broader applications of this classic algorithm across various fields.
elements.sent-2411-092: DBSCAN excels in detecting clusters of arbitrary shapes and identifying noise, making it a robust choice for diverse clustering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-093: However, its efficiency can be significantly impacted by computational limitations.
elements.sent-2411-094: Specifically, the neighborhood search step, which requires calculating pairwise distances for each point, can become a bottleneck.
elements.sent-2411-095: This issue is exacerbated in large-scale datasets, where the sheer volume of points amplifies the computational burden, and in high-dimensional spaces, where distance computations are inherently more resource-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-096: For instance, in artificially generated datasets such as two-moons and two-circles, the clustering results of $k$-means applied directly in the original feature space differ significantly from those in the spectrally-embedded feature space, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:nonconvexshape .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \subfigure[$k$-means on the orig space of the two circles data set]{ \includegraphics[width=1.5in,height=1in]{twomoonsKmeans.eps} } \subfigure[$k$-means on the spectrally-embedded space of the two circles data set ]{ \includegraphics[width=1.5in,height=1in]{twomoonsSC.eps} } \subfigure[$k$-means on the orig space the two circles data set]{ \includegraphics[width=1.5in,height=1in]{twocircleKmeans.eps} } \subfigure[$k$-means on the spectrally-embedded space of two circles data set]{ \includegraphics[width=1.5in,height=1in]{twocircleSC.eps} } \caption{Clustering results of $k$-means on the original and spectral-embedded feature spaces.} \label{fig:nonconvexshape}
elements.sent-2411-097: To leverage spectral graph theory for analyzing the key structural information of a dataset, we first construct a graph from the data.
elements.sent-2411-098: For example, a k-nearest neighbor (kNN) graph can be used to provide a basic representation of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-099: The graph is then embedded into an $r$-dimensional space by utilizing the first $r$ eigenvectors of its Laplacian matrix, a process widely known as spectral graph embedding~\cite{belkin2003laplacian .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Following this, we compute the similarity between data points in the spectral-embedded space, which captures the structural information of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-101: Next, the samples are divided into subsets such that samples within the same subset exhibit high spectral correlation.
elements.sent-2411-102: The mean of the feature vectors within each subset is then calculated to create a spectrally representative pseudo-sample.
elements.sent-2411-103: These pseudo-samples collectively form a compressed representation of the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-104: If further compression is required, the pseudo-samples can be iteratively compressed again.
elements.sent-2411-105: The spectral similarity between two points, $u$ and $v$, is defined as follows~\cite{livne2012lean : \begin{equation}s_{uv} := \frac{\left|(\mathbf{X}_u, \mathbf{X}_v)\right|^2}{(\mathbf{X}_u, \mathbf{X}_u)(\mathbf{X}_v, \mathbf{X}_v)}, \quad (\mathbf{X}_u,\mathbf{X}_v) := \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbf{x}_u^{(k)} \cdot \mathbf{x}_v^{(k)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-106: In this formulation, $\mathbf{x _u$ and $\mathbf{x _v$ represent the feature vectors of points $u$ and $v$ in the spectral embedding space.}}
elements.sent-2411-107: From a statistical perspective, $s_{uv}$ quantifies the proportion of variance accounted for by the linear regression of $\mathbf{x _u$ on $\mathbf{x _v$ in a spectral context.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Higher values of $s_{uv}$ signify a stronger spectral association between the two points.
elements.sent-2411-109: The DBSCAN algorithm is then applied to the compressed data points, grouping them into distinct clusters.
elements.sent-2411-110: Subsequently, each original data point inherits the cluster membership of its corresponding compressed point, thereby finalizing the clustering process.
elements.sent-2411-111: Based on our observations, when the data dimensionality is excessively high (e.g., several hundred dimensions), neither the original DBSCAN algorithm nor our proposed method can achieve high-precision clustering results.
elements.sent-2411-112: In this paper, we retained the original dimensionality of the Pendigits dataset.
elements.sent-2411-113: For the MNIST dataset, we first reduced its dimensionality from 256 to 16 using UMAP, implemented in Python.
elements.sent-2411-114: For the dimensionally reduced data, we used MATLAB's inbuilt DBSCAN implementation to perform the clustering.
elements.figure-2411-005: \begin{center} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Data Set Orig Ours(2X) Ours(5X) Ours(10X) Pendigits 62.50 62.50 77.94 75.91 MNIST 69.09 68.85 78.43 77.82 \end{tabular}} \end{center} \caption{Clustering Quality Results (accuracy)} \label{table:nmi_result}
elements.sent-2411-115: As illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:acc_dbscan.eps , the proposed approach maintains consistently high clustering quality as the compression ratio increases, highlighting its robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Furthermore, Fig. \ref{fig:runtime_dbscan.eps demonstrates that our method achieves significant speed enhancements for DBSCAN algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In addition to these improvements, the method greatly enhances memory and storage efficiency, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained environments.
elements.sent-2411-118: These advancements pave the way for handling substantially larger datasets on energy-efficient computing platforms, such as FPGAs or handheld devices, emphasizing the transformative potential of the proposed method.

elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{runtime_dbscan.eps} \caption{DBSCAN time VS compression ratio for the MNIST data set.\protect\label{fig:runtime_dbscan.eps}}
elements.sent-2411-119: In DBSCAN, outlier detection and clustering are achieved simultaneously during the same execution.
elements.sent-2411-120: Specifically, the DBSCAN algorithm assigns a cluster label of -1 to the detected outliers.
elements.sent-2411-121: Therefore, after obtaining the clustering results on the compressed dataset using our method, the labels of the compressed points with a value of -1 are mapped back to the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-122: This mapping allows us to derive the outlier detection results on the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-123: In the absence of ground truth labels for outliers, we utilized the Jaccard similarity coefficient to compare the anomaly detection results.
elements.sent-2411-124: The Jaccard similarity coefficient is a statistical measure that evaluates the similarity and diversity between two sets.
elements.sent-2411-125: It is defined as the size of the intersection divided by the size of the union of the two sets: \begin{equation}\text{Jaccard Similarity} = \frac{\left| A \cap B \right|}{\left| A \cup B \right|}\end{equation} where \( A \) and \( B \) are the sets of detected outlier indices from each algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-126: The coefficient ranges from \(0\) to \(1\), with a value of \(0\) indicating no overlap between the sets (completely dissimilar), and a value of \(1\) indicating that the sets are identical (completely similar).
elements.sent-2411-127: Values closer to \(1\) signify a higher degree of similarity between the sets, while values closer to \(0\) indicate greater dissimilarity.
elements.sent-2411-128: For the Pendigits dataset, the Jaccard Similarity Coefficient between the outlier detection results obtained through 10X compression and the results from MATLAB's in-built standard DBSCAN algorithm is 0.7978.
elements.sent-2411-129: Experiments on large-scale, real-world data sets demonstrate that the proposed method can significantly improve the performance of DBSCAN algorithm.
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{center} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Data Set Orig Ours(2X) Ours(5X) Ours(10X) Pendigits 62.1831 62.5967 63.0638 68.5615 USPS 81.5552 87.7178 80.3936 79.9419 MNIST 68.5257 78.6400 78.4257 79.7214 \end{tabular}} \end{center} \caption{Clustering Accuracy} \label{table:acc_result}
